inbound.subject: Starting over after a layoff
inbound.body: Hi coach, I want to get back to running after a messy few months. My big goal is to get healthy enough to race a half marathon again this spring. Right now I mostly need help rebuilding without doing something stupid.

outbound.subject: Re: Starting over after a layoff
outbound.text:
Hi — glad you reached out. Getting back after a messy few months is exactly the kind of work I like to help with, and your priority of rebuilding ‘without doing something stupid’ is the right approach.
You want a cautious rebuild so you can race a half marathon in spring while avoiding setbacks — that’s exactly what we’ll plan for during this week-1 return block.
Before I write a safe, personalised week-1 plan I need a few quick details so I can match load to your current readiness. Please reply with short answers to the numbered questions below:
1) Time availability — how many days/week can you realistically run, and which day is best for your long run? Any weekday time windows (e.g., mornings 6–7am, evenings after 6pm)?
2) Current injury/health status — any pain right now, recent injuries, or medical/physio advice I should know about? Any medications that affect heart rate or recovery?
3) Recent training — approx. weekly mileage for each of the last 4 weeks (or an estimate), and the longest continuous run you completed recently.
4) Current run ability — can you run continuously now? If so, how long and at what perceived effort (easy/moderate)? Do you use a walk–run pattern?
5) Race timing — do you have a target race date or a rough month in spring?
6) Other constraints — sleep, work/travel, kids, access to pool/bike/gym, preferred surface (road/trail), and what shoes you usually use.
Next steps once you reply:
- I’ll send a conservative, repeatable week-1 plan tailored to your answers and a short rationale for why I chose that structure.
- I’ll tell you what I need you to report after each session so I can tweak load safely (quick items: duration or distance, perceived effort, any pain or unusual soreness, and how you felt later that day).
Communication cadence: I’ll prepare the week-1 plan after your reply and I recommend either a quick check-in after each run via message or a single end-of-week summary — which do you prefer (email or short weekly messages)? If you’d rather talk it through, I can do a 15–20 minute call to go over details now.
Reply with those six short answers and your preferred check-in method and I’ll send the plan.
Coach

inbound.subject: A little more context
inbound.body: A bit more context: I used to run consistently before the layoff and I'd call myself intermediate. I can train four days a week most weeks if we keep it realistic. The thing that nags me is mild Achilles tightness when I get greedy. I also do better when the plan feels structured.

outbound.subject: Re: A little more context
outbound.text:
Thanks — this extra context is really helpful. I hear: intermediate runner, can do ~4 days/week if we keep increases conservative, goal is to rebuild consistency and eventually race a half, and mild Achilles tightness that flares when you "get greedy." 
Brief reflection
You’ve got a solid base historically, want a structured but realistic 4x/week plan, and the Achilles is the main limiter. We’re in Week 1 of your return-safely block. Your job this week is consistency and symptom monitoring; my job is to keep progression slow and clear.
Week 1 (risk-managed) — session-by-session
- Session 1 — Easy aerobic: 30–40 minutes, conversational pace. Focus on upright posture and a light cadence. No pickups.
- Session 2 — Strength (20–30 minutes): low-load, controlled work with a calf-control emphasis — single-leg balance, slow calf raises, and targeted glute work. Keep reps deliberate; stop before fatigue compromises form.
- Session 3 — Easy aerobic: 25–35 minutes easy. If the Achilles feels off, swap this for cross-training (bike/elliptical) or an easy recovery walk.
- Session 4 — Short controlled tempo (OPTIONAL): 15–20 minutes steady effort total (include easy warmup). Only attempt this if you’ve had no morning Achilles pain for 7–10 days and both easy runs felt normal. If not, replace with an easy run or repeat the strength session.
Safety rules & stop criteria (be explicit)
- If Achilles pain increases during a session, stop intensity immediately and switch the session to easy/recovery or cross-train. Tell me right away.
- If pain is worse the next morning or lasts >24 hours, remove intensity from the following sessions and revert to the easiest anchor sessions (the two easy runs and the low-load strength).
- No hard sessions when risk is red-tier. Use symptom control and consistency as the primary filter.
Why this structure
We’re rebuilding rhythm first while protecting the tendon: limit intensity, keep sessions short, and add low-load strength early to support the Achilles. We’ll progress one training lever at a time (frequency → duration → intensity).
What I need from you now
Please reply with short answers to these: your availability across the week (days you prefer), recent weekly mileage and your longest recent run, any pacing or fitness markers you track (easy pace, recent tempos, etc.), target race timing (if you have one), and preferred check-in method. Also tell me how the first two easy sessions feel this week (pain during, pain next morning).
Coaching cadence & expectations
I’ll check in weekly and adjust. Please message me immediately after any symptom change or after the optional tempo if you do it. If Week 1 completes cleanly, we’ll repeat this structure once more and then add one small progression (either a slightly longer easy run or a longer strength session).
Next-step contingencies (clear rules)
- If both weeks are green and repeatable: move to a controlled_load_progression.
- If any flare-ups occur: keep the Week 1 pattern or reduce load further until symptoms settle.
Quick final note: success this week = completing the planned easy runs and strength session without an increase in Achilles symptoms. Keep things conservative and report any change.
